lesson:
Before Google Maps, getting around in Japan required 地図 in a way most Western countries didn't experience. Japanese addresses aren't sequential — buildings aren't numbered in order along a street. A street address tells you the district, block, and building number, but finding the actual building often required a hand-drawn map. 地図を書いてください (please draw me a map) was once one of the most practical phrases in the language. Restaurants printed mini maps on their business cards. Wedding invitations included them.

The kanji: 地 (earth, land) + 図 (drawing, diagram). A drawing of the land. 地図を見る (look at a map). 世界地図 (world map). 地図アプリ (map app).

図 shows up wherever something is being schematized: 図書館 (library), 図形 (shape/figure), 設計図 (blueprint). The kanji means "to map out" — whether it's land, a plan, or an idea.

運 (carry, transport, luck) + 転 (turn, roll, revolve). Carrying by turning — wheels turning, moving forward. The word covers the operation of any vehicle: 車を運転する (drive a car), バスを運転する (drive a bus), 電車を運転する (operate a train).

運転免許 (うんてんめんきょ, driver's license) — shortened in casual speech to just 免許. 運転手 (うんてんしゅ, driver). 飲酒運転 (いんしゅうんてん, drunk driving). The word appears in compound after compound because driving is a central part of daily life.

Beyond vehicles, 運転 also means the operation of any machinery or system: 運転資金 (operating capital), 運転を再開する (resume operations — said of train lines after a delay). When a train line is disrupted, the announcement says 運転を見合わせています (operations are suspended).

meaning: to get off bus/train, go down, descend, land
lesson:
Getting on is 乗る. Getting off is 降りる. These two bracket every ride you'll ever take in Japan. 次の駅で降りる (get off at the next station). バスを降りる (get off the bus). 飛行機が降りる (the plane descends/lands).

The kanji 降 has the mound radical (阝) on the left — originally depicting a hill or earthen wall — and components suggesting downward movement on the right. Things come down: rain falls (降る, ふる), you step down (降りる). The shared kanji connects weather falling from the sky with people stepping down from vehicles — both are descents.

降りる vs 下りる: Both are read おりる and both mean "to descend." 降りる emphasizes getting off/out of something (vehicles, stages). 下りる emphasizes going physically downward (stairs, mountains). In practice, the distinction is blurry and many Japanese speakers use them interchangeably.

Particles: 降りる takes を for the vehicle you exit — 電車を降りる (get off the train). This を marks the starting point of movement, not a direct object. Where you get off takes で — 渋谷で降りる.

Passive に降りられる: rare but possible. 途中で客に降りられた (the passenger got off on me mid-ride — a taxi driver's complaint).

Causative を／に降りさせる: を for coercive — making someone get off. 酔っ払いをタクシーから降りさせる (make the drunk person get out of the taxi).

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 降ろす (おろす)
Dictionaryおりるを
Negativeおりないを
Politeおりますを
Polite negおりませんを
Pastおりたを
Polite pastおりましたを
て-formおりてを
Volitionalおりようを
Polite volおりましょうを
Passiveおりられるに
Causativeおりさせるを／に
Caus-passおりさせられるに
Potentialおりられるを
Imperativeおりろを
Imp なさいおりなさいを
Cond ～ばおりればを
Cond ～たらおりたらを

From English/Dutch "gas" — one of the older loanwords in Japanese, entering during the Meiji period when Western technology flooded in. In daily life, ガス almost always means cooking gas or heating gas — the stuff piped into your home. ガスをつける (turn on the gas). ガスを止める (shut off the gas). ガス代 (がすだい, gas bill).

In Japan, moving into a new apartment means calling three utility companies: 電気 (electricity), ガス (gas), and 水道 (water). ガス is always in that trinity. ガス会社 (gas company). ガスコンロ (gas stove). ガス漏れ (がすもれ, gas leak) — a term seared into the national consciousness after the 1995 subway sarin attack, though that involved a different kind of gas entirely.

For gasoline (car fuel), Japanese uses ガソリン, not ガス. That's a common English speaker's mistake — asking for ガス at a gas station would be confusing.

必ず is a promise compressed into two syllables. It doesn't mean "always" in the statistical sense (いつも handles that). It means "without fail" — a guarantee. 必ず来てね (make sure you come). 必ず成功する (it will definitely succeed). 約束は必ず守る (I always keep my promises — not as a habit, as a commitment).

The kanji 必 looks like a heart (心) pierced by a line — something driven through the center with absolute certainty. The character means "inevitable, certain, without fail" and shows up in compounds that share that iron certainty: 必要 (ひつよう, necessary), 必死 (ひっし, desperate — literally "certain death").

In practice, 必ず often appears in instructions and warnings: 必ず確認してください (be sure to check). It's the word that turns a suggestion into an imperative without raising your voice.

From English "camera," but the word has been in Japanese long enough to feel fully naturalized. カメラ covers every kind: デジタルカメラ (digital camera, shortened to デジカメ), ビデオカメラ (video camera), 防犯カメラ (ぼうはんカメラ, security camera).

Japan's camera industry shaped the global market. Canon, Nikon, Sony, Fujifilm, Olympus — all Japanese. カメラマン means "photographer" or "cameraman" in Japanese (another loanword compound). The word is deeply embedded in a country where photography is practically a national pastime — cherry blossom season turns every park into a sea of cameras.

The phone camera has shifted the word's center of gravity. カメラを向ける (point a camera at someone) used to mean a physical device; now it often means a phone. カメラで撮る (take a photo with a camera) is giving way to スマホで撮る. But カメラ remains the default word for the device itself.

meaning: to commute, go regularly, frequent a place
lesson:
通う is about regular trips back and forth — a path you travel repeatedly. Not a one-time journey (that's 行く), but a routine. 学校に通う (go to school — every day, as a student). 病院に通う (go to the hospital regularly — for ongoing treatment). ジムに通う (go to the gym regularly). The word implies commitment: you're not visiting once, you're making it part of your life.

The kanji 通 is the movement radical (辶) with 甬 — a passageway. Something that goes through, passes through, connects. It shows up in 通る (とおる, to pass through), 通り (とおり, street), 交通 (こうつう, traffic). The difference: 通る is a single passage, 通う is the repeated version — going through the same passage again and again.

Particles: 通う takes に for the destination — the place you go to regularly. 塾に通う (attend cram school).

Passive に通われる: rare, but can appear in honorific speech — 先生はこの道場に通われていた (the teacher used to attend this dojo — respectful).

Causative を／に通わせる: を for coercive, に for permissive. 子供を塾に通わせる (have the kid attend cram school) — one of the most commonly heard uses of this causative in Japan.

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryかように
Negativeかよわないに
Politeかよいますに
Polite negかよいませんに
Pastかよったに
Polite pastかよいましたに
て-formかよってに
Volitionalかよおうに
Polite volかよいましょうに
Passiveかよわれるに
Causativeかよわせるを／に
Caus-passかよわせられるに
Potentialかよえるに
Imperativeかよえに
Imp なさいかよいなさいに
Cond ～ばかよえばに
Cond ～たらかよったらに

急 means "sudden, urgent, steep" — and に turns it into an adverb. 急に雨が降ってきた (it suddenly started raining). 急に寒くなった (it got cold all of a sudden). The word captures a change that arrives without warning — one moment things are normal, the next they're different.

The kanji 急 has 心 (heart) at the bottom and components suggesting grabbing or pressing at the top. Something seizing your heart — that jolt when circumstances shift without preparation. It appears in 急行 (きゅうこう, express train), 緊急 (きんきゅう, emergency), 急ぐ (いそぐ, to hurry). Speed, urgency, abruptness — always that sense of no time to spare.

急に vs 突然 (とつぜん): both mean "suddenly" but 突然 is more dramatic, more literary. 急に is the everyday version — something changed fast. 突然 is for when the door slams open in a horror movie.

"Salary" + "man" — a Japanese-English compound (和製英語) that doesn't exist in actual English. It means a white-collar office worker, typically male, employed full-time at a company. The word carries an entire lifestyle: long hours, commuter trains, dark suits, after-work drinking with colleagues, loyalty to one company for an entire career. Whether that image is admired or pitied depends on who's talking.

The term has become somewhat loaded. It can be neutral — 父はサラリーマンです (my father is an office worker) — or it can imply a faceless cog in the corporate machine, someone who traded individuality for stability. Japanese media often contrasts サラリーマン with entrepreneurs or freelancers as a way of questioning traditional career paths.

For women, the equivalent was OL (office lady), though that term is increasingly seen as outdated and reductive. The gender-neutral 会社員 (かいしゃいん, company employee) is the safer, more formal alternative for any gender.

In Japan, the 25th of the month is 給料日 (きゅうりょうび, payday) for most workers. Restaurants near office districts get busier that evening. The phrase 給料日前 (before payday) is shorthand for being broke — those last few days when the wallet is thin and lunch options narrow.

給 (supply) + 料 (fee): what's supplied to you for your work. 給料が出る (get paid — literally "salary comes out"). 給料が安い (the pay is low). Talking about money openly is still somewhat taboo in Japan, but 給料 is the word people reach for when they do.

Neighborhood: 給料 is everyday. 給与 (きゅうよ) is formal — tax documents and HR departments. 月給 (げっきゅう) is monthly salary specifically. 年収 (ねんしゅう) is annual income. The more formal the word, the less likely you are to hear it in conversation.

meaning: piece of music, tune, song, melody
lesson:
The kanji 曲 originally meant "bent, curved" — and it still carries that meaning in 曲がる (まがる, to turn/bend). How did "bent" become "song"? The connection is the winding, turning quality of melody — a tune doesn't go straight, it curves up and down, bends through intervals. A piece of music is a curve of sound.

この曲が好き (I like this song). 新曲 (しんきょく, new song/track). 名曲 (めいきょく, masterpiece/famous piece). 曲 also serves as a counter for songs: 三曲 (three songs/tracks).

Note that 曲 and 歌 (うた, song) overlap but aren't identical. 歌 emphasizes singing — a song with vocals. 曲 is broader — any piece of music, including instrumentals. A Beethoven symphony is a 曲 but not really a 歌. A pop song is both.

meaning: to be cut, break, run out, cut well
lesson:
切れる is the intransitive partner of 切る (きる, to cut). Where 切る is you cutting something, 切れる is something getting cut, breaking, or running out — on its own, without your hand on the blade.

The word branches widely. 紐が切れた (the string broke). 電池が切れた (the battery died — ran out). 期限が切れた (the deadline expired). このナイフはよく切れる (this knife cuts well). That last usage is different — "cuts well" describes the knife's ability, not something breaking. Context always tells you which 切れる you're dealing with.

There's also an idiomatic sense: 頭が切れる (sharp-minded, clever) — a mind that cuts through problems. And キレる (written in katakana) means "to snap, lose one's temper" — a slang usage that became mainstream. The thread connecting all these: a sharp break, a clean separation.

Particles: 切れる takes が — the thing that breaks or runs out is the subject. 電池が切れた. パンが切れている (we're out of bread).

Passive: Already intransitive, so passive is essentially unused.

Causative を／に切れさせる: rare. You'd normally use 切る (transitive) instead of forcing 切れる into a causative.

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 切る (きる)

The kanji 正 is 一 (one) over 止 (stop). The original meaning: to go straight toward a goal without deviating — to stop at the one correct place. From there: correct, right, proper, just. It's one of those kanji whose meaning is so foundational it appears everywhere: 正月 (New Year), 正午 (noon), 正式 (formal/official), 正直 (honest).

正しい covers both factual correctness and moral rightness. 正しい答え (the correct answer). 正しい判断 (the right decision). 正しいことをする (do the right thing). English needs two different words — "correct" for facts, "righteous" for morality — but Japanese uses one. The assumption baked into the word: truth and goodness are the same thing.

The sound ta-da-shi-i is long — four morae — which gives it a weighty, authoritative feel in speech. When someone says 正しい, it sounds like a verdict being delivered.

Dictionaryただしい
Negativeただしくない
Pastただしかった
Past negただしくなかった
Adverbただしく
て-formただしくて

苦しい is physical and emotional pain at the same time. 息が苦しい (it's hard to breathe). 生活が苦しい (life is hard / financially tight). 苦しい言い訳 (a strained excuse). The word describes the feeling of being squeezed — something pressing on you that you can't escape.

The kanji 苦 has the plant radical (艹) on top — it originally referred to a bitter-tasting plant. Bitterness extended to suffering: the taste of hardship. 苦い (にがい) still means "bitter" for tastes, while 苦しい is the suffering version. Same kanji, two different adjective forms, two related but distinct meanings.

苦しい vs 痛い (いたい, painful): 痛い is sharp, localized pain — you stubbed your toe, your head hurts. 苦しい is prolonged, whole-body distress — you can't breathe, you're struggling, the weight is too much. 痛い makes you flinch. 苦しい makes you endure.

Dictionaryくるしい
Negativeくるしくない
Pastくるしかった
Past negくるしくなかった
Adverbくるしく
て-formくるしくて

細かい lives in the space between "small" and "detailed." Small change: 細かいお金. Fine print: 細かい字. Detailed instructions: 細かい説明. A meticulous person: 細かい人. The connecting idea: things broken down into tiny, precise pieces.

That last usage — 細かい人 — is a double-edged compliment. It can mean someone who's thorough and detail-oriented, or someone who's fussy and nitpicky. そんな細かいこと気にしないで (don't worry about such minor details) leans negative. 細かいところまで気が付く (notices even the fine details) leans positive. Context and tone decide.

The kanji 細 has the thread radical (糸) — something thin and fine. 細い (ほそい) means physically thin or slender. 細かい means small/detailed. Both share the kanji because both involve fineness — thin things and tiny things are cousins.

Dictionaryこまかい
Negativeこまかくない
Pastこまかかった
Past negこまかくなかった
Adverbこまかく
て-formこまかくて

The kanji 静 is 青 (blue/green) + 争 (conflict, dispute). An odd combination — conflict plus a color? The folk reading: when the fighting stops and everything settles into a clear blue calm, that's 静. Peace after conflict. Stillness after noise. Whether or not that's historically accurate, the image works: the blue sky after a storm.

静かにしてください (please be quiet) — you will hear this in libraries, on trains, from parents to children. It's the polite way to ask for silence. 静かな朝 (a quiet morning). 静かに暮らす (live quietly). The word covers both literal silence and a calm, undisturbed state of being.

静か vs うるさい (noisy, annoying) — direct opposites that Japanese people toggle between constantly. A 静かな neighborhood is desirable. うるさい neighbors are a problem. The contrast is binary in a way that reflects Japanese sensitivity to noise — shared walls, thin houses, crowded trains make quietness a social virtue.

Dictionaryしずか（な）
Negativeしずかじゃない
Pastしずかだった
Past negしずかじゃなかった
Adverbしずかに
て-formしずかで

健 (robust, healthy) + 康 (peaceful, at ease). A body that's strong and at ease — health. The word works as both a noun and a な-adjective: 健康が大事 (health is important) and 健康な体 (a healthy body).

Japan is obsessed with 健康 in a way that shapes daily life. 健康診断 (けんこうしんだん, health checkup) is mandatory for most workers once a year. 健康食品 (health foods) fill entire aisles in convenience stores. TV shows dedicate segments to 健康法 (health methods). The word isn't abstract — it's a daily pursuit with specific rituals: morning walks, balanced meals, regular checkups, hot baths.

A common greeting: お体に気をつけて (take care of yourself — literally, be careful with your body). Health isn't just a personal matter in Japan. Wishing someone good health is a social act, especially toward elders. 健康 is what you wish for others and work toward for yourself.

Dictionaryけんこう（な）
Negativeけんこうじゃない
Pastけんこうだった
Past negけんこうじゃなかった
Adverbけんこうに
て-formけんこうで

From English "golf." The sport arrived in Japan in 1903 and became a corporate religion. ゴルフ in Japan is less a weekend hobby than a business ritual — playing a round with clients or superiors is a career move. 接待ゴルフ (せったいゴルフ, business entertaining golf) is a compound word that exists because the practice is so common it needed a name.

That said, golf's role has been shifting. Course membership fees that once cost millions of yen have dropped. Younger Japanese are less drawn to the corporate golf culture. But the word itself remains common: ゴルフ場 (ごるふじょう, golf course), ゴルフクラブ (golf club — both the equipment and the organization).

ゴルフをする or ゴルフに行く — both are natural. The first emphasizes playing, the second emphasizes going. If someone invites you with 今度ゴルフ行きませんか, they're suggesting an outing, not just a game.

From English "course," but Japanese uses it more broadly than you might expect. マラソンコース (marathon course/route). ゴルフコース (golf course). ランチコース (lunch course — a set meal with multiple dishes). That last one catches English speakers off guard: a コース at a restaurant means a prix fixe meal, not a main course.

コース料理 (course meal, full-course dinner) is the restaurant usage you'll encounter most. Aコース and Bコース on a menu mean "set A" and "set B" — different packages at different price points. The word implies a structured sequence: appetizer, main, dessert, flowing one after another.

In education: 文系コース (humanities track), 理系コース (science track). In life planning: 人生のコース (life course/path). The word always implies a predetermined route or sequence — something laid out for you to follow.

meaning: to request, ask, beg, order (food/drink), entrust
lesson:
頼む is the everyday word for asking someone to do something for you. Not asking a question (聞く), not requesting formally (お願いする) — just asking, person to person: can you do this? 友達に頼む (ask a friend). 仕事を頼む (ask someone to do work).

At restaurants, 頼む means "to order": ビールを頼む (order a beer). It's more casual than 注文する (ちゅうもんする), which is the formal "place an order." Between friends at an izakaya, もう一杯頼もう (let's order another) is natural.

The one-word exclamation 頼む！ (or 頼むよ) means "I'm begging you" or "come on, please" — the kind of thing you say when you really need someone to come through. 頼むから静かにして (I'm begging you, please be quiet). It's direct, slightly desperate, and very human.

The kanji 頼 has 束 (bundle) + 頁 (head/page). The idea of bowing your head in a bundle of obligation — relying on someone. 頼る (たよる, to rely on) shares the same kanji.

Particles: 頼む takes を for the thing requested and に for the person asked. 友達に引っ越しを頼む (ask a friend to help move). At a restaurant, just を: ビールを頼む.

Potential が頼める: が marks what you can order/request. ここでランチが頼める (you can order lunch here).

Passive に頼まれる: に marks who asked you. 上司に頼まれた (my boss asked me to do it). Very common — being asked to do things by others is a constant.

Causative に頼ませる: making someone request. Uncommon — you'd normally just make the request yourself.

五段 Godan他動詞

Politeたのみますを
Polite negたのみませんを

て-formたのんでを

Causativeたのませるに

Cond ～ばたのめばを

meaning: near, close to; in the near future, before long
lesson:
You already met 近い (ちかい) — the adjective meaning "close." 近く is what happens when you shift it into noun/adverb territory. Drop the い, add く, and the word stops describing something and starts being a place or a time: 駅の近く (near the station — a location). 近くに住んでいる (I live nearby).

The time meaning sneaks up on people. 近く届くと思います (I think it will arrive before long). Here 近く doesn't point to a physical spot — it points to a temporal one, the near future. Same logic: something is close, whether in space or in time.

This noun/adverb shift — い-adjective → く form as a standalone word — is a pattern worth watching for. 遠い → 遠く (far away, a distant place). 多い → 多く (many, a large number). The く form doesn't always become an independent word, but when it does, it's always a noun describing the quality itself or the place/amount defined by it.

甘い means sweet — but Japanese sweetness has a longer reach than English. Sugar is 甘い. A parent who spoils a child is 甘い. A grading rubric that lets everyone pass is 甘い. An overly optimistic plan is 甘い. The thread connecting them: softness where there should be firmness. Too easy, too lenient, too indulgent. When someone says 考えが甘い (your thinking is naive), they're saying you've sugared over the hard parts.

甘える (あまえる) — the verb form — is one of those Japanese concepts with no clean English equivalent. It means to depend on someone's kindness, to act spoiled, to let yourself be indulged. Babies 甘える with their mothers. Adults sometimes 甘える with partners. The psychologist Takeo Doi wrote an entire book (The Anatomy of Dependence) arguing that 甘え is the key to understanding Japanese social behavior.

The kanji 甘 is thought to depict something held in the mouth — a horizontal line inside the mouth radical. Taste it and know it. Sweet on the tongue, soft at the core.

Dictionaryあまい
Negativeあまくない
Pastあまかった
Past negあまくなかった
Adverbあまく
て-formあまくて

Japanese had tables long before this word arrived. The ちゃぶ台 (ちゃぶだい) — a low table you sit around on the floor — is so culturally iconic that there's a gesture named after it: ちゃぶ台返し (flipping the table in anger), immortalized in the anime Star of the Giants and still used as a metaphor for losing your temper and destroying everything. テーブル is its Western replacement — a table with legs, paired with chairs instead of floor cushions. When Japan imported Western furniture during modernization, the word came with it.

The split tells you about the setting. A ちゃぶ台 or 卓 (たく) lives in a tatami room. A テーブル lives in a Western-style room with a hard floor. In compound territory, the native word still dominates: 食卓 (しょくたく, dining table), 卓球 (たっきゅう, table tennis — literally "table ball"). テーブル is the loanword that marks which era of furniture you're sitting at.

A compound you can decode on sight: 食べ (eat, from 食べる) + 物 (もの, thing) = eating-thing = food. This 〜物 pattern is productive. 飲み物 (のみもの, drink), 買い物 (かいもの, shopping), 忘れ物 (わすれもの, forgotten item). Verb stem + もの = the thing associated with that action. Once you see the pattern, you can often guess new compounds before looking them up.

食べ物 vs ご飯 vs 料理: Three words for "food" that aren't interchangeable. 食べ物 is the broadest — anything edible, food as a category. ご飯 (ごはん) means a meal or cooked rice — it's what you sit down to eat. 料理 (りょうり) is prepared cuisine, food as something cooked with skill. 好きな食べ物は？ (what food do you like?) is asking about your taste in general. ご飯食べた？ (did you eat?) is asking about a specific meal.

You already know 始める (はじめる) — "to start something." 始まる is its intransitive partner: things start on their own. 映画が始まる (the movie starts). 授業が始まった (class has begun). Nobody starts the movie in this sentence — it just begins. That's the whole difference: 始める needs someone pulling the trigger. 始まる just happens.

This める/まる pair is one of the most common transitive/intransitive patterns in Japanese. 決める/決まる (decide/be decided), 集める/集まる (gather something/things gather). If you see a verb ending in める, look for a まる partner — chances are good it exists.

Particles: 始まる takes が — the thing that begins is the subject, not an object you're acting on. 会議が始まる (the meeting begins). Nothing starts it; it starts.

Passive が始まられる: extremely rare for intransitive 始まる. If you need passive, use 始められる (passive of transitive 始める) instead.

Causative を／に始まらせる: also rare. Japanese naturally uses 始めさせる (causative of 始める) when you want to make someone start something.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 始める (はじめる)

Negativeはじまらないが

From English "game," but in Japanese ゲーム has drifted toward a narrower default. Say ゲーム without context and most Japanese people will picture a video game first, a board game second, and a sports match barely at all. For sports, Japanese uses 試合 (しあい). For children playing, 遊び (あそび). ゲーム has carved out its own territory: structured play with rules, especially electronic.

ゲームをする (play a game). ゲームセンター (game center — arcade). ゲーム機 (げーむき, game console — literally "game machine"). Japan's relationship with games needs no introduction — the country that produced Nintendo, Sony PlayStation, and most of the RPGs the world grew up on. When a Japanese person says ゲームばかりしている (all they do is play games), it's a complaint you'll hear from parents and partners across the country.

A cross: 十. One horizontal line, one vertical. The simplest kanji in the entire writing system. Why a cross for ten? In ancient Chinese tally systems, ten was marked with a vertical stroke through a horizontal one — a complete set, all fingers counted. The character has barely changed in three thousand years because there's nothing to simplify.

じゅう is the Chinese reading. You'll also encounter the native Japanese とお for the standalone number ten, though じゅう dominates in compounds and counting: 十一 (じゅういち, eleven), 十分 (じっぷん, ten minutes — note the sound change), 二十 (にじゅう, twenty). The whole number system above ten is just stacking: 二十 = 2×10, 三十 = 3×10, 百 (100), 千 (1000). Ten is the first building block you need.

Sound trap: 十 before certain consonants compresses to じっ or じゅっ: 十分 can be じっぷん or じゅっぷん (both accepted). 十回 (じっかい, ten times). This is just Japanese smoothing out awkward consonant clusters — say じゅうかい fast enough and it naturally collapses.

天 (heaven, sky) + 気 (spirit, air, energy) = the mood of the sky. Japanese packages weather not as a meteorological report but as the sky's temperament on a given day. 天気がいい (the weather is good). 天気が悪い (the weather is bad). Notice the adjectives: good and bad, not warm and cold. 天気 gets judged morally, like a person's mood.

Weather talk in Japanese is as socially essential as it is in English. いい天気ですね is the Swiss Army knife of small talk — safe, universally usable, and signals that you're a normal human who acknowledges shared experience. If you're ever stuck for a conversation opener, this is it.

The kanji 天 is a person (大) with a line above their head — what's above a person is the sky, the heavens. 気 originally depicted steam rising from cooking rice — visible energy, breath, atmosphere. Weather is the energy radiating from the heavens. The etymology lines up.

Japanese has three different あつい — three words that sound identical but mean completely different things, written with different kanji. This is the one that'll matter most from June through September.

暑い — hot weather. The air around you is hot. 今日は暑い (it's hot today).
熱い — hot to touch. A surface, a liquid, a thing that burns. このコーヒーは熱い.
厚い — thick. A book, a wall, a steak. Completely unrelated meaning, same sound.

The kanji tells you which is which. 暑 has 日 (sun) at the top — the sun beating down, environmental heat. 熱 has the fire radical — direct, physical heat from a source. If you're complaining about summer, you want 暑い. If you're warning someone about their soup, you want 熱い. Mix them up and people will know exactly what you mean, but the kanji distinction reveals how Japanese carves up the concept of heat differently from English.

Dictionaryあつい
Negativeあつくない
Pastあつかった
Past negあつくなかった
Adverbあつく
て-formあつくて

太い describes girth — something that's wide around, not just big. A thick rope, a fat marker pen, a stout tree trunk. 太い腕 (thick arms). 線が太い (the line is thick). It's physical bulk measured across, not up or out.

The kanji 太 is 大 (big) with a dot added. That single dot is the difference between "big" and "thick" — a small addition that changes the shape of bigness from general to specifically round and full. The verb 太る (ふとる, to gain weight) comes from the same root, and it's the word you'd use for a person getting heavier. Calling a person 太い directly is blunt — it's closer to "fat" than "heavyset." Like in English, there are gentler ways to say it, and native speakers usually choose them.

太い vs 厚い vs 細い: Dimensions in Japanese get specific. 太い is thick around (circumference). 厚い (あつい) is thick through (depth — a thick book, a thick wall). 細い (ほそい) is the opposite of 太い — thin, slender, narrow.

Dictionaryふとい
Negativeふとくない
Pastふとかった
Past negふとくなかった
Adverbふとく
て-formふとくて

晩 is evening — specifically the early-to-mid evening, the hours after work when you eat dinner and wind down. It overlaps with 夜 (よる, night) but skews earlier. 今晩 (こんばん, tonight/this evening) is one of the first compounds you'll use: こんばんは (good evening). 晩ご飯 (ばんごはん) is dinner — the evening meal.

The kanji 晩 has 日 (sun) on the left and 免 on the right. The sun is departing, slipping away. That trailing quality is baked into how 晩 feels — not the deep dark of 夜, but the transition into it. In literary Japanese, 晩年 (ばんねん) means the later years of someone's life, the evening of their time. Same word, scaled up from hours to decades.

土 (earth, soil) + 曜日 (day of the week) = Earth-day = Saturday. The Japanese days of the week follow the same celestial body system as the Western ones, which shouldn't be a coincidence — it isn't. Both trace back to the same Hellenistic astrological tradition that assigned planets to days. The system entered Japan via China around the 9th century.

Saturday = Saturn's day in English, and Saturn was associated with the element earth in East Asian cosmology. So 土曜日 and "Saturday" are distant cousins, connected through the same planet. The full set: 日 (Sun), 月 (Moon), 火 (Mars/fire), 水 (Mercury/water), 木 (Jupiter/wood), 金 (Venus/metal), 土 (Saturn/earth).

土 as a kanji is a cross planted in the ground — a stake marking where the earth is. It's one of the five classical elements, and you'll see it in 土地 (とち, land), 土産 (みやげ, souvenir — literally "earth-product," a gift from the place you visited). Saturday carries that grounded energy.

The word you'll need at the doctor, the dentist, and the gym. 頭が痛い (my head hurts). お腹が痛い (my stomach hurts). The pattern is always [body part] が 痛い — the body part is the grammatical subject, the thing that's in a state of pain. Not "I have a headache" but "my head is painful." Japanese centers the body part, not the sufferer.

Beyond physical pain, 痛い has developed a slang meaning in modern Japanese that's worth knowing: embarrassingly clueless. あの人、ちょっと痛いよね (that person is kind of cringe). Someone who reads the room wrong, who tries too hard, who doesn't realize how they come across — that's 痛い. The logic: watching them makes you wince, as if in pain.

The kanji 痛 has the sickness radical 疒 (that slanted roof with dots — you'll see it on many medical/health kanji) enclosing 甬, a phonetic component. Any time you see 疒 wrapping a kanji, expect something related to illness or physical distress.

Dictionaryいたい
Negativeいたくない
Pastいたかった
Past negいたくなかった
Adverbいたく
て-formいたくて

The polite, everyday word for father — what you'd call your own dad at home, and what you'd use when referring to someone else's father in conversation. お (honorific prefix) + 父さん. Compare with 父 (ちち), the humble form you'd use when talking about your own father to outsiders. This inside/outside distinction runs through all Japanese family terms: お母さん/母, お兄さん/兄. You elevate their family, you humble your own.

But here's what trips people up: you also call your own father お父さん when speaking to him or about him within the family. お父さん、ご飯できたよ (Dad, dinner's ready). It's not humble or polite in that context — it's just what "Dad" sounds like in Japanese. The humble form 父 only activates when you're talking about him to outsiders: 父は会社員です (my father is a company employee).

Younger kids often say パパ. Rougher, more masculine speech uses 親父 (おやじ) — closer to "old man" in tone.

多分 sits at about 70-80% certainty. Not "definitely." Not "who knows." It's the word for when you're fairly sure but don't want to commit. 多分大丈夫 (probably fine). 多分来ると思う (I think they'll probably come).

The kanji literally says 多 (many) + 分 (parts) — "for the most part." That etymology maps perfectly onto the meaning: the majority of the evidence points this way, but not all of it. It's hedged confidence.

Certainty scale: Japanese has a gradient of commitment that's useful to place 多分 on. From most to least certain: 絶対 (ぜったい, absolutely) → きっと (surely, ~90%) → 多分 (probably, ~70%) → もしかしたら (possibly, ~30%). Japanese conversation lives in the 多分 zone — definitive statements feel aggressive, so speakers naturally gravitate toward this comfortable middle ground.

時 (time) + 計 (measure, count) = a device that measures time. Wall clock, wristwatch, alarm clock — all 時計. Unlike English, which splits these into separate words, Japanese uses one and lets context or a prefix sort it out: 腕時計 (うでどけい, wristwatch — arm-clock), 目覚まし時計 (めざましどけい, alarm clock — wake-up clock), 置き時計 (おきどけい, desk clock — placed clock).

Notice the voicing: standalone it's とけい, but in compounds the と softens to どけい. This is 連濁 (rendaku) — the same sound-smoothing process that turns 花 (はな) into 生け花 (いけばな). No rule governs when rendaku happens and when it doesn't. It's one of those things you absorb through exposure.

The reading とけい is a slightly unusual compound reading — you might expect じけい from the standard on'yomi. The word's pronunciation came from a different historical path and just stuck.

Three verbs pronounced とまる. Three different kanji. Three different meanings. This is the one about sleeping somewhere that isn't your home.

泊まる — to stay overnight (at a hotel, a friend's place). Has the water radical 氵, originally connected to boats mooring in a harbor — a vessel stopping for the night.
止まる — to stop, cease (movement ends).
停まる — to stop (vehicles pulling over, parking).

When you hear とまる, the overnight meaning is usually clear from context — hotels, friends' houses, travel plans. ホテルに泊まる (stay at a hotel). 友達の家に泊まる (stay at a friend's house). The particle に marks where you stay — the destination of your overnight presence.

Particles: 泊まる takes に — the place you stay at. 旅館に泊まる (stay at a ryokan). You can add a duration with counter: 二泊する (stay two nights) uses the noun form 泊 (はく) with する.

Passive に泊まられる: someone staying at your place uninvited or inconveniently. 友達に三日も泊まられた (my friend stayed over for three whole days — and I had to deal with it). Classic suffering passive.

Causative を／に泊まらせる: letting or making someone stay over. に for permissive: 友達に泊まらせる (let a friend stay over).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 泊める (とめる)

どうして literally breaks down as どう (how) + して (doing) — "doing how?" → "how did this come about?" → "why?" The path from "how" to "why" is a short one: if you're asking how something happened, you're really asking why it happened. English does this too — "how come?" means "why?"

Three ways to ask "why" in Japanese, each at a different register:
なぜ — formal, written, stiff. News reports, academic papers.
どうして — middle ground. Conversational but not rough. Carries a slight emotional charge — genuine puzzlement or frustration.
なんで — casual, everyday. The one friends use with each other.

The emotional coloring matters. どうして来なかったの？ (why didn't you come?) sounds like you actually want to know and are a little hurt. なんで来なかったの？ is more neutral — maybe annoyed, maybe just curious. どうして is the one that can carry disappointment without sounding confrontational.

meaning: to hang, put on (direct object, eg glasses)
lesson:
掛ける is one of the most overloaded verbs in Japanese. Its core meaning is "to hang" or "to apply something onto something else," but from that root it branches into a dozen specific uses. 壁に絵を掛ける (hang a picture on the wall). 眼鏡を掛ける (put on glasses — hang them on your face). 電話を掛ける (make a phone call — hang your voice on the line). 鍵を掛ける (lock a door — apply the lock). 声を掛ける (call out to someone — throw your voice at them).

The thread: you're placing something onto or over something else. Glasses onto your face. A lock onto a door. Your voice onto a person. Words onto a telephone line. Once you see 掛ける as "apply X to Y," the different uses stop feeling random.

The kanji 掛 has the hand radical 扌 — this is an action done with your hands — plus 卦, which is a phonetic component related to divination sticks being hung up. The hand physically hanging something: that's the image at the character's core.

Particles: 掛ける takes を for the thing being hung/applied, and often に for the surface or target. 壁に絵を掛ける. 友達に電話を掛ける.

Potential が掛けられる: が — what can be hung/applied becomes the subject.

Passive に掛けられる: に marks the agent. 夜中に電話を掛けられた (someone called me in the middle of the night — I suffered).

Causative に…を掛けさせる: に marks who you make do the hanging, を marks the thing.

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 掛かる (かかる)

meaning: to turn, bend, curve (no direct object)
lesson:
曲がる is the verb of turning — streets, roads, your body. 右に曲がる (turn right). 角を曲がる (turn the corner). This is the verb you need for navigation: 次の信号を右に曲がってください (please turn right at the next traffic light). It's intransitive — the road curves, you turn, the wire bends. Nobody is bending it (that would be 曲げる, the transitive partner).

The kanji 曲 depicts something that was once straight and has been bent — an angular shape with a curve forced into it. It also means "song" or "musical piece" (この曲が好き, I like this song), which seems unrelated until you realize that melodies bend and curve too — a tune is a line that doesn't stay straight.

Particles: Two patterns. を marks the path or corner you turn at: 角を曲がる (turn the corner). This is movement-を, marking the space traversed, not a direct object. に marks the direction: 左に曲がる (turn left).

Passive に曲がられる: rare and unusual for this verb.

Causative を／に曲がらせる: making someone turn. 運転手にここで曲がらせた (I had the driver turn here).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 曲げる (まげる)

The polite way to say stomach. 腹 (はら) is the bare kanji reading — blunt, masculine, used in set expressions. お腹 wraps it in the honorific お and softens the reading to なか, making it the standard everyday word. You'd always say お腹 in polite company. 腹 is for tough-guy talk and idioms.

Two phrases you'll use constantly: お腹が空いた (I'm hungry — my stomach has emptied) and お腹がいっぱい (I'm full — my stomach is packed). Notice the verb 空く (すく) — it means "to become empty," and the past tense 空いた describes the resulting state. Your stomach emptied and now it's in that state. Japanese frames hunger as an event that happened to your stomach, not a feeling you're experiencing.

The kanji 腹 has the meat/body radical 月 (which looks like "moon" but in body-part kanji represents flesh) plus 复, a phonetic element meaning "return" or "repeat." The belly is where things go and come back — where food enters and energy returns from.

Borrowed from English, but ミーティング occupies a specific niche that native Japanese words don't quite fill. Japanese already has 会議 (かいぎ) for formal meetings — board meetings, committee meetings, anything with an agenda and minutes. ミーティング is lighter: a team check-in, a quick sync, a casual gathering to discuss something. It implies less hierarchy and less formality.

This split is common with loanwords. Japanese doesn't borrow English words randomly — it borrows them to fill gaps in nuance or register. 会議 feels corporate and official. ミーティング feels modern and approachable. A startup would more naturally schedule a ミーティング. A government ministry would hold a 会議. Same activity, different cultural packaging.

朝のミーティング (morning meeting). ミーティングがある (I have a meeting). ミーティングルーム (meeting room) — yet another layer of English bolted on.

嫌い looks like an い-adjective but isn't. It's a な-adjective: 嫌いな食べ物 (food I dislike), not 嫌い食べ物. The い at the end is a trap — it's part of the word's root, not the adjective ending. A few な-adjectives do this (きれい, 嫌い), and you just have to memorize which ones.

Grammatically, 嫌い works like 好き — the mirror image. The thing you dislike takes が: 野菜が嫌い (I dislike vegetables). Intensify it: 大嫌い (だいきらい, hate). The scale runs: 大好き → 好き → 嫌い → 大嫌い.

Saying 嫌い directly to someone is harsh. あなたが嫌い is a devastating sentence — romantic breakup territory. For softer rejection, Japanese has an arsenal of indirectness: ちょっと苦手 (にがて, "I'm not great with it"), あまり好きじゃない ("I don't really like it"). The culture of indirectness means 嫌い hits harder than its English equivalent.

Dictionaryきらい
Negativeきらいじゃない
Pastきらいだった
Past negきらいじゃなかった
Adverbきらいに
て-formきらいで

金 (metal, gold) + 曜日 = Metal-day = Friday. In the planetary day system, Friday belongs to Venus (just like in English — Friday comes from the Norse goddess Freya, associated with Venus). In East Asian five-element cosmology, Venus corresponds to metal. So 金曜日 and "Friday" both point to Venus through different cultural lenses.

金 by itself means gold or money (お金, おかね, money). The association makes Friday feel like payday, and in modern Japan it kind of is — 花金 (はなきん, "flower Friday") was a Bubble-era term for the celebration of Friday night after getting paid. The term has faded but the sentiment hasn't. Friday evening in any Japanese city is busy.

Mnemonic: Friday is kin-youbi. You kin-da want the weekend to start. Or: payday is on Friday, and you're making that 金 (money).

Pronounced exactly like いる (to exist/be, for animate things) — but completely different word, different kanji, different conjugation pattern. いる (to be) is ichidan. 要る (to need) is godan. In conversation you can't tell them apart by sound, only by context: お金が要る (I need money) vs. 猫がいる (there's a cat).

In practice, 要る is most commonly heard in its negative form: 要らない (いらない, don't need it). At a shop: レシート要りますか？ (do you need a receipt?) → 要りません or the casual 要らない. The negative conjugation is the giveaway that this is godan, not ichidan — いない (not there) vs. いらない (don't need).

The kanji 要 means "essential" or "main point" — it shows up in 重要 (じゅうよう, important) and 必要 (ひつよう, necessary). When something is 要る, it's not just wanted — it's required.

Particles: 要る takes が — the needed thing is the subject. パスポートが要る (a passport is needed). Not を — the thing isn't an object you're acting on, it's a subject that's in a state of being necessary.

Passive/Causative: Rarely used for this verb. Japanese reaches for 必要とされる or 求められる when it needs a passive "is needed" construction.

五段 Godan他動詞

ない is simultaneously the simplest and most important word you'll learn. It means "there isn't" or "doesn't exist" for inanimate things — the counterpart to ある. お金がない (there's no money). 時間がない (there's no time). But that's just its surface job.

The deeper role: ない is the negative ending bolted onto adjectives, verbs, and entire sentences. 食べない (don't eat). 高くない (not expensive). 静かじゃない (not quiet). Every negative statement in Japanese ends in ない or one of its conjugated forms. It's the word of absence, and it's everywhere.

Grammatically, ない behaves like an い-adjective. It conjugates: なかった (past), なくて (て-form), なければ (conditional). This means every negative verb is essentially an adjective at its tail — 食べない conjugates further as 食べなかった, 食べなくて, etc. Understanding that ない is an adjective, not a suffix, unlocks how Japanese negation works at every level.

Dictionaryない
Negative(N/A — already negative)
Pastなかった
Past neg(N/A)
Adverbなく
て-formなくて

Most Japanese colors aren't adjectives. You can say 赤い (red), 白い (white), 黒い (black), 青い (blue) — these ancient colors have い-adjective forms. But yellow had to hack its way in. The base word is the noun 黄色 (きいろ, yellow color), and someone stuck い on the end to force it into adjective shape: 黄色い. It works, everyone uses it, but it's structurally a noun with an adjective costume on.

Both forms coexist: 黄色い花 and 黄色の花 both mean "yellow flower" and both sound natural. 茶色い (ちゃいろい, brown) does the same trick. These are the only two colors that pulled off this noun-to-adjective conversion. Every other non-basic color stays as a noun with の: 緑の, 紫の, ピンクの.

The kanji 黄 is visually busy — it originally depicted the color of firelight or a particular type of jade. 色 (いろ) means color. Together: yellow-color. Clean and literal.

Dictionaryきいろい
Negativeきいろくない
Pastきいろかった
Past negきいろくなかった
Adverbきいろく
て-formきいろくて

Same sound as 風 (かぜ, wind) — and that's not a coincidence. In traditional East Asian medicine, colds were caused by "wind" entering the body. 風邪 literally writes out as 風 (wind) + 邪 (evil, perverse) = evil wind. A cold is a malicious breeze that got inside you. The same logic exists in Chinese medicine, where 風邪 (fēngxié) refers to pathogenic wind. English has a faint echo of this too — we say you "catch" a cold, as if something airborne grabbed you.

風邪を引く (to catch a cold) — note the verb: 引く, to pull/draw. You don't "get" a cold in Japanese. You "draw" it in, like pulling something toward you. 風邪を引いた (I caught a cold). 風邪気味 (かぜぎみ, coming down with a cold — cold-ish).

In Japan, wearing a face mask when you're sick predates COVID by decades. Saying 風邪を引いたので (because I caught a cold...) is a socially acceptable reason for almost anything — canceling plans, leaving early, wearing a mask. The common cold does real social work in Japanese culture.

Two words pronounced やさしい. Two completely different kanji. This is the one about the heart.

優しい — kind, gentle, tender. The kanji 優 has the person radical and 憂 (grief, sorrow). A person who knows grief — who has felt sorrow and therefore treats others softly. Kindness born from understanding pain. That etymology is beautiful and worth remembering: 優しい people aren't kind because they're naive. They're kind because they know what hurting feels like.

易しい — easy, simple. The kanji 易 means "easy" or "change." Completely different meaning, same pronunciation.

Context always makes the distinction clear. 優しい人 (a kind person). 易しい問題 (an easy problem). In speech, you'll never confuse them because one describes people and the other describes tasks. But it's worth noting both exist so you don't freeze when you see やさしい written in hiragana and have to read the context to know which one it is.

Dictionaryやさしい
Negativeやさしくない
Pastやさしかった
Past negやさしくなかった
Adverbやさしく
て-formやさしくて

meaning: to become sunny, clear up, be sunny
lesson:
晴れる is the sky clearing. Clouds part, sun comes through, the world brightens. 明日は晴れるかな (will it clear up tomorrow, I wonder). It's intransitive — nobody makes the sky clear; it clears on its own. The noun form 晴れ (はれ) is what you'll see on weather forecasts for sunny weather.

But 晴れる isn't limited to meteorology. 気持ちが晴れる (my feelings cleared up — I feel better). 疑いが晴れる (suspicion is cleared). The metaphor is universal: clarity after obscurity, relief after burden. When something that was weighing on you lifts, it 晴れる.

The kanji 晴 is 日 (sun) + 青 (blue). Sun plus blue — blue skies. It's one of the most visually poetic kanji, describing the exact thing you see when the weather is fine. The compound 晴天 (せいてん, fine weather) doubles down on the image.

Particles: 晴れる takes が — the sky or weather is the subject that clears. 空が晴れる (the sky clears). Often used without an explicit subject when the context is obviously weather.

Passive/Causative: Almost never used — you can't be passively cleared-up or make something sunny. The verb's nature is purely spontaneous.
